title: On the trail of the honey badger (Extension passage)
content_html:
On the trail of the honey badger (Extension passage)

On a recent field trip to the Kalahari Desert, a team of researchers learnt a lot more about honey badgers. The team employed a local wildlife expert, Kitso Khama, to help them locate and follow the badgers across the desert. Their main aim was to study the badgers' movements and behaviour as discreetly as possible, without frightening them away or causing them to change their natural behaviour.

'The problem with honey badgers is they are naturally curious animals, especially when they see something new,' says Khama. 'That, combined with their unpredictable nature, can be a dangerous mixture. They're actually quite sociable creatures around humans, but as soon as they feel they might be in danger, they can become extremely vicious.'

The research confirmed many things that were already known. As expected, honey badgers ate any creatures they could catch and kill. Even poisonous snakes, feared and avoided by most other animals, were not safe from them. The researchers were surprised, however, by the animal's fondness for local melons, probably because of their high water content. The team also learnt that, contrary to previous research findings, the badgers occasionally formed loose family groups.

Following some of the male badgers was a challenge, since they can cover large distances in a short space of time. Some hunting territories cover more than 500 square kilometres. Although they seem happy to share these territories with other males, there are occasional fights over an important food source.

options: To find where honey badgers live.
To observe how honey badgers behave.
To try to change the way honey badgers behave.
To temporarily catch some honey badgers
To find out why honey badgers have such a bad reputation.
questions.question: Why did the wildlife expert visit the Kalahari desert? (Choose TWO letters A–E)
questions.explanation:
(Đoạn 2): "Their main aim was to study the badgers’ movements and behaviour as discreetly as possible..." (Mục đích chính của họ là nghiên cứu di chuyển và hành vi của rái cá mật một cách kín đáo nhất có thể).

Từ "observe" (quan sát) đồng nghĩa với "study" (nghiên cứu), và "how they behave" chính là "behaviour".
Đoạn D ("To temporarily catch some honey badgers" - Để bắt tạm thời một vài con rái cá mật) là vì ở đoạn 2 có câu:"They also planned to trap a few and study them close up before releasing them.""trap a few" = bắt một vài con (bằng bẫy)."before releasing them" = trước khi thả chúng đi ->hành động bắt này chỉ mang tính tạm thời (temporarily).

_blockConfig.noteContent:
**A type of parrot:**
- diet consists of fern fronds, various parts of a tree and [1]
- nests are created in [2] where eggs are laid.
**Arrival of settlers**
- the [3] of the kākāpō were used to make clothes.

options: They show interest in things they are not familiar with.
It’s hard to tell how they will behave.
They are always looking for food.
They do not enjoy human company.
It is common for them to attack people.
questions.question: What two things does Kitso Khama say about honey badgers? (Choose TWO letters A–E)
questions.explanation: EN: Khama states they are 'naturally curious... especially when they see something new' (show interest in unfamiliar things) and have an 'unpredictable nature' (hard to tell how they behave). VI: Khama phát biểu rằng chúng 'tự nhiên tò mò... đặc biệt khi thấy cái gì đó mới' (quan tâm đồ vật lạ) và có 'bản tính khó đoán' (khó biết chúng cư xử thế nào).

options: There are some creatures they will not eat.
They were afraid of poisonous creatures.
They may get some of the water they need from fruit.
They do not always live alone.
Female badgers do not mix with male badgers.
questions.question: What two things did the team find out about honey badgers? (Choose TWO letters A–E)
questions.explanation: EN: They found badgers have a 'fondness for local melons, probably because of their high water content' (get water from fruit) and 'occasionally formed loose family groups' (don't always live alone). VI: Họ phát hiện lửng 'ưa thích dưa hấu địa phương, có lẽ vì hàm lượng nước cao của chúng' (lấy nước từ trái cây) và 'thỉnh thoảng thành lập các nhóm gia đình lỏng lẻo' (không phải lúc nào cũng sống một mình).

options: They don’t run very quickly.
They hunt over a very large area.
They defend their territory from other badgers.
They sometimes fight each other.
They are more aggressive than females.
questions.question: Which two features are typical of male badgers? (Choose TWO letters A–E)
questions.explanation: EN: Male territories 'cover more than 500 square kilometres' (large area) and although they share territories, 'there are occasional fights' (sometimes fight each other). VI: Lãnh thổ của con đực 'bao phủ hơn 500 km vuông' (diện tích lớn) và dù họ chia sẻ lãnh thổ, 'thỉnh thoảng vẫn xảy ra các cuộc ẩu đả' (đôi khi đánh nhau).

options: The badgers lost interest in people.
The badgers became less aggressive towards other creatures.
The badgers started eating more.
Other animals started working with the badgers.
Other animals near them became more relaxed.
questions.question: What two things happened when badgers got used to humans? (Choose TWO letters A–E)
questions.explanation: EN: When badgers get habituated to humans, they lose interest in them, and other wild animals close to them show relaxed behavior. VI: Khi loài lửng quen với con người, chúng mất đi sự tò mò/hứng thú với họ, và các động vật hoang dã khác gần chúng cũng thể hiện hành vi thư thái hơn.
